summary: Growing up in New York City in the 1930s was quite unique for me as a Nisei kid. There was very little discrimination, and I had friends from various backgrounds including Italian Americans and Germans. My family settled there after my grandfather stowed away on an American freighter and jumped ship in New York. It was a place where I learned to live bilingual and multicultural, which shaped my identity in many ways.

Japan was the homeland my family came from, specifically from Fukuoka Prefecture. During World War II, I was sent there as part of the Military Intelligence Service during the Occupation of Japan. It was a difficult time, especially seeing the aftermath of bombings in cities like Tokyo, Nagasaki, and Hiroshima. My role was to help bridge the gap between the Americans and Japanese, despite the suspicion from locals seeing us in American uniforms.

My grandfather was originally from Fukuoka Prefecture in Japan. He was the youngest of five brothers and decided to leave because he didn’t want to be a slave to his older siblings. His decision to stow away on an American freighter and jump ship in New York changed our family’s destiny. Fukuoka is where our roots lie, but New York became our new home.

Interestingly, my dad became friends with the head of the Italian Mafia in New York City. This connection was so strong that when my grandfather was detained at Ellis Island, the Mafia’s influence helped get him released quickly. Growing up, I even had a close Italian American friend whose family was involved with the Mafia, which made me feel like I was part Italian sometimes. Their power and community presence were quite impactful in our lives.

John Gotti was the boss of the Italian Mafia, and my dad was friends with him. One of my Italian American friends eventually became John Gotti’s counselor. This connection was part of my unique upbringing in New York City, where I was exposed to different cultures and communities, including the Mafia. It was a strange but fascinating part of my childhood.

Ellis Island was where my grandfather was detained during World War II because he was considered an enemy alien and a Japanese community leader. Thankfully, when my dad informed his Mafia friends about this, my grandfather was released the very next day. It showed me how powerful connections could be, especially during those tense times. Ellis Island was more than just an immigration station; it was also a place of detention for some like my grandfather.

Being a Nisei growing up in Queens in the 1930s was a unique experience. I was bilingual and raised in a traditional Japanese household, but I didn’t fully identify as Japanese until I met other Nisei from the West Coast, camps, and Hawaii during basic training. They taught me what it meant to be proud of my heritage. Later, as part of the Military Intelligence Service, I saw firsthand the importance of Nisei in bridging cultures during the war and occupation.

Many of the Nisei I met during basic training were from Hawaii, and they helped me understand what it meant to be Japanese American. Unlike me, who grew up in New York City, they had different experiences, especially with the internment camps on the West Coast. Their stories and pride in being Nisei helped me embrace my identity and realize the importance of our role during the war.

I was assigned to the Military Intelligence Service Japanese language school after basic training, where I learned to read and write Japanese and about Japanese culture. Serving in MIS during the war and occupation was a critical role, as we were the bridge between the American forces and the Japanese people. It was tough work, especially dealing with suspicion from Japanese locals, but it was vital for a peaceful occupation. MIS saved lives and helped ease tensions after the war.

I was initially expecting to be sent to Europe with the 442nd Infantry Regiment, which was a segregated unit of Japanese American soldiers famous for their bravery. But instead, I was assigned to the Military Intelligence Service. Looking back, I think that probably saved my life because I was very gung-ho and might have been killed in combat. The 442nd was highly respected, and I admired their courage deeply.

After my training with the Military Intelligence Service, I was deployed to the Philippines during World War II. It was part of the Pacific campaign, and from there I eventually went on to participate in the Occupation of Japan. The Philippines was a key location where we prepared for the next phase of the war and post-war activities. It was a challenging but important assignment.

I was part of the Occupation of Japan after World War II as a member of the Military Intelligence Service. We were tasked with helping to rebuild and stabilize Japan, and it was a difficult job given the devastation in cities like Tokyo, Nagasaki, and Hiroshima. We often had to deal with suspicion from the Japanese people, but our work was crucial in preventing the occupation from becoming a horror like in Germany. General Douglas MacArthur recognized the importance of Nisei like me in this effort.

Nagasaki was one of the cities devastated by the atomic bomb during World War II, and I saw the terrible aftermath during the Occupation of Japan. The suffering was immense, with many children wandering the streets without parents or proper clothing. It was heartbreaking, and despite orders not to aid the enemy, we did what we could to help these innocent victims. Nagasaki’s destruction was a stark reminder of the war’s human cost.

Like Nagasaki, Hiroshima was also devastated by the atomic bomb, and I witnessed the terrible conditions there during the Occupation of Japan. The city was in ruins, and many civilians suffered greatly. It was a sobering experience that made me realize the true cost of war. Our role in the occupation was to help rebuild and bring some measure of peace to these shattered communities.

During the Occupation of Japan, I was stationed in Tokyo, where we took over the old Nippon Yusen Kaisha Shipping Company near the main station. Tokyo had been heavily firebombed, and the destruction was severe. We worked in teams to manage the occupation duties, often dealing with Japanese soldiers speaking in dialects and a population suspicious of us. It was a tough but vital assignment to help restore order in the capital.

I served in the United States Army during World War II, initially going through basic training and then being assigned to the Military Intelligence Service. The Army placed me in a segregated unit with other Nisei soldiers, which was a unique experience. My role was critical in the Pacific theater and the Occupation of Japan, where I helped bridge cultural gaps and gather intelligence. Serving in the Army was both challenging and a source of pride for me and my family.

General Douglas MacArthur was the supreme commander during the Occupation of Japan, and he understood the importance of Nisei soldiers like me. He made sure that the occupying forces treated us with respect and recognized our unique role as cultural and language bridges. MacArthur’s leadership was crucial in making the occupation more effective and less brutal than it could have been. His chief advisor, Tagami, was also a key figure in this effort.

Tagami was one of General MacArthur’s chief advisors during the Occupation of Japan. He was a Kibei Nisei, meaning he was born in the U.S. but educated in Japan, and he was an excellent person to have on our side. Tagami helped guide the occupation policies and was respected by both the American forces and the Japanese people. His presence made a big difference in how the occupation was conducted.

During the Occupation of Japan, we took over the old Nippon Yusen Kaisha Shipping Company building near the main Tokyo station. It was a significant location where about 3,000 of us were stationed before being assigned to various parts of Japan. The building symbolized the transition from Japanese control to American occupation. Working there was part of the larger effort to stabilize and rebuild post-war Japan.

entity: United States Department of Veterans Affairs
summary: Many years after the war, I began experiencing nightmares about my time during the Occupation of Japan, which led to a diagnosis of post-traumatic stress syndrome. The United States Department of Veterans Affairs has been treating me for this condition. It’s ironic and difficult that events from over 70 years ago still affect me today, but the VA’s support has been important in managing these challenges. I’m grateful for their help as I continue to cope with my memories.
